name: Woodland Seasonal Round
description: The seasonal cycle of the eastern woodland nations — following the rhythms of the Great Lakes, St. Lawrence, and Atlantic watersheds. Heritage skills are practiced in ecological context: the right time, the right material, the right protocol.

seasons.name: Sugar Moon (Ziisbaakdoke Giizis / Siikoobimizii Giizis)
ecologicalMarkers: Maple sap rises when nights are below freezing and days above
First crow sightings announce spring is coming
Snow begins to crust (good for snowshoe travel)
activities: Maple sap tapping and collection
Sugar camp setup and family gathering
Maple syrup and maple sugar production
Spring fishing begins as rivers open
Snowshoe travel at maximum efficiency on crusted snow
seasons.culturalContext: The sugar bush season is a major community gathering time. Families traveled from winter hunting camps to established sugar bush sites. For the Anishinaabe, this was one of the first major food production events of the year after a long winter on cached and hunted food.

seasons.name: Planting Moon / Fishing Moon
ecologicalMarkers: Ice breaks up on lakes and rivers
Fiddleheads emerge from forest floor
Wild leeks (ramps) carpet the forest floor
Walleye and perch spawn in shallow water
activities: Three Sisters planting begins (corn, beans, squash) — Haudenosaunee
Fiddlehead and wild leek harvesting
Spring fishing — walleye, perch, suckers running upstream
Birchbark harvesting begins (birch sap is running, bark peels cleanly)
Cedar bark harvesting for weaving and construction
Canoe repair after winter storage — birchbark patching
seasons.culturalContext: Spring is the season of renewal and the most intensive planting and harvesting period for shoreline and forest peoples. Birchbark at this time of year is at its most pliable and easiest to harvest without damaging the tree.

seasons.name: Berry Moon / Green Corn Moon
ecologicalMarkers: Wild blueberries, raspberries, and strawberries ripen
Thunderstorm season
Green corn reaches harvest stage — Haudenosaunee
Wild rice (manoomin) heads begin to fill — Anishinaabe
activities: Wild berry picking and preservation (drying, making pemican-style berry mixtures)
Green Corn Ceremony preparation — Haudenosaunee (Level 2-3)
Fishing continues; smoking fish for storage
Hide tanning begins — hides taken in spring are processed now
Woodworking projects — carving, canoe building in summer camp
Gathering medicinal plants at peak potency
seasons.culturalContext: Midsummer is a time of abundance and extended community gatherings. The berry season provided critical vitamin C after winter. For the Haudenosaunee, the Green Corn Ceremony is a major thanksgiving gathering (Level 2 — educational framing required, ceremonial specifics not included).

seasons.name: Ricing Moon (Manoominike Giizis) / Harvest Moon
ecologicalMarkers: Wild rice (manoomin) heads are ripe — tested by rubbing a head between palms
First cool nights signal approaching autumn
Geese and ducks begin to gather for migration
Corn, beans, and squash reach full harvest stage
activities: Wild rice (manoomin) harvesting — canoe-based, using ricing sticks to knock heads — Anishinaabe (Level 1 for technique, Level 2 for protocols)
Wild rice parching and winnowing
Three Sisters harvest — corn, beans, squash — Haudenosaunee
Corn drying and hominy preparation
Hunting begins — deer and moose
Smoking and drying meat and fish for winter storage
Basswood bark harvesting for fiber
seasons.culturalContext: The wild rice harvest is one of the most significant seasonal events in Anishinaabe culture — spiritually, economically, and nutritionally. The manoomin camp was a community gathering equivalent in importance to the sugar bush. For the Haudenosaunee, the Three Sisters harvest marks the end of the agricultural year.

seasons.name: Hunting Moon / Freezing Moon
ecologicalMarkers: Deciduous trees drop leaves — game is more visible
First frosts arrive; freeze-up begins on ponds and small lakes
Moose and deer in rut — most active and visible
Migrating waterfowl move through
activities: Intensive moose and deer hunting
Moose hide preparation and tanning
Preparing winter shelter — checking for drafts, reinforcing wiigiwaam or longhouse
Final food storage preparations for winter
Snowshoe construction and repair for approaching winter
Net fishing under newly forming ice
Gathering and storing firewood
seasons.culturalContext: The fall hunt was critical for winter food security. A successful moose hunt provided hundreds of pounds of meat, hide for clothing, and bone for tools. Communities worked together to process large kills efficiently.

seasons.name: Winter Moon / Story Moon
ecologicalMarkers: Deep snow — snowshoe travel essential
Ice thick enough for ice fishing
Extreme cold — survival skills fully deployed
Long nights — primary storytelling season
activities: Ice fishing under the ice using jigging techniques and tip-ups
Trapping — setting and checking trap lines for beaver, muskrat, otter, marten
Woodworking projects — carving, snowshoe frame construction
Hide tanning using brain-tan techniques during cold weather
Weaving and sewing — moccasins, clothing, baskets during long indoor periods
Storytelling — winter is the season for teaching through story (cultural protocols around winter storytelling apply)
seasons.culturalContext: Winter in the woodland nations was a time of relative stillness. The major food-getting activities were ice fishing and trapping. Long indoor periods were used for craft production and cultural transmission through storytelling. In Anishinaabe tradition, certain stories can only be told when there is snow on the ground.
